speaker: interviewer
text: It's tape number six, interview with Regina Mauskopf. If we can get back to Guben.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --when we-- from Ravensbruck, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they got the-- the-- the girls there. And we were a lot of with frozen legs. Oh, my, I don't even know what happened to them. I-- there were two girls, two sisters, that when they were going to work-- that I heard that already after the-- the liberation-- that as they-- I don't know how they befriended a gentile-- a German. And while that thing was going on, they somehow escaped. And they were working in the fields where the Germans-- as not Jewish, kinda. And they came home. That's-- I heard about those two girls afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there-- and taking those potatoes. And, again, I went-- while they were not burning at least-- because they were raw. And I used to pack in real a lot, because nobody cared already what's, you know, what I'm-- you're taking out from the kitchen, what you're doing, you know. Well, they cared, but as I was the personnel from the kitchen too, so they didn't watch me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS were kind of-- they were burning papers. They were busy with their own things. They had buckets and burning papers outside from the-- but they were-- we had the gate from the-- the camp. And then they had there about two or three barracks. They were SS women. One of them was very good. She called me and wants to clean her house-- her little thing. She gave me very good mashed potatoes. And she gave me a pair of white knee socks-- this German. So-- so I went in a few times in the evening. And when I came out, I just untied the raffia. And the potatoes were falling. And they would grab.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they kept on hollering every night. How many times did they see me in the daytime? Kinda, you know, and they were-- they called me the potato girl in Hungarian-- the potato girl who had-- come on. When is it here? You wanna be able to-- to-- to give? So I don't know how long, if they-- we were there, over like that-- until they organized the whole evacuation from there. And-- Approximately how long would you say were you in Guben?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Guben-- February the 8th we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- that was-- that was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh, this I remember good. I just don't remember before the holidays we were there already. But they were-- she was a Hungarian, a mother with a daughter. I don't know from where she got a-- a sitter. A lot of was it the mothers that I don't know. But-- and she could-- by heart, she was doing that-- that-- that-- that woman. And he kind of sneaked in there a little bit, kind of, when I was learning things at Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. I don't remember Yom Kippur. We were fasting. That's for sure. But we were there. And-- so-- and then we just kind of marched, kind of like, hundred per five and hundred. And 1/2--

speaker: interviewer
text: From-- from Guben?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Guben. And every-- every hundred, by the two sides, there were-- on horses-- the soldiers with that shield here, that silver shield what they were wearing. I don't know what rank that is or what-- what-- whatever they wore. And we were marching I don't know how many days. And then they put us on trains. But in the trains, we were staying more in the train than-- I think because the soldiers, the-- come from the front, from Russia-- what-- that kind of, you know, there's a feet or soldiers kind of sticking out through windows. You know, and we were so-- because we were in our train. And in-- in the other, you know, track, they were-- they were staying in theirs-- by those trains. And we knew that they're dead, because they did not-- they staying there all-- we were staying here a whole day almost, and they were staying in the same position there, kind of like, you know, we saw far from the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- did everyone survive the march to the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from our girls, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see any?

speaker: interviewee
text: But not from the Ravensbruck. I don't know. Just those which we knew from our-- from before the Ravensbruck girls came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice whether anyone collapsed from the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if they would collapse?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just shot them-- got shot. Everybody said, don't you go in there, even if you need-- you know, we would need to go. Should-- well, they, said, do it wherever you're walking. You're doing-- because once you go in the gutter, you have been shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you're marching through the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, for the nights, they-- especially if they were bombing-- was going on terribly. So we were in by the farmers in the stables. They put us up. One day there I was beaten up there too, from an SS woman already. Because we were there for kind of like a day or maybe more. We were sewing. I took out my things, and we took two blankets. Because in Guben here, everybody had two blankets. So I made myself a pair of like ski things, you know, with thing. And then I borrowed for other girls, did the same thing-- whatever we could just in that one day that they're doing. It was Shabbas on top on that. And he was telling us, well, it's Shabbas, but let's do it. And that's it. And so all of a sudden, somebody came in and they said, ooh, they're feeding the pigs outside. And somebody came in with-- with-- just with their hands full of those potatoes with that cornmeal, whatever. And naturally, I run out, too. And I start to kind of grab in their thing. And-- and the SS woman-- by that time, the SS woman found out what we are doing. So I was a little bit too late for those potatoes to thing. So I was beat up a little bit again. And it's-- and-- and-- and that was that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were then-- they put us then on trains. As I said, I don't know, was it before those potatoes or after those? But we just stayed there. And then they needed the trains. So we were out of the train. Because our train was going just maybe a 1/2 a mile this way and then a 1/2 a mile back. Because they had to let through trains. So we were out again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were marching too. And it was raining. The blanket got wet when we come to Bergen-- to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- how long all together did you march, if you consider--

speaker: interviewee
text: About two weeks, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive the two weeks of marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- see, it's-- it's-- it's the willpower. It's the help. Yeah. Sometimes, when they let us, kind of like, they wanted to rest, the soldiers. So they led us by the fields-- they led us. And there was those-- it was, like, yams growing. And we were kind of running around there. They didn't care. I thought, you know-- a thing. And we were taking from the ground, whatever was left, I think, from-- through the winter, you know, not picked out or whatever. I don't know why they were there, because it was March, something like that. I don't even remember one place I was in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kind of shoes you were wearing during the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: My own.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still had your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were wet, and moist, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wet, that-- that hasn't-- it didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your health--

speaker: interviewee
text: --oh, how many times you got wet on the zahlappell stand-- not just in-- in-- in-- in any camp. We got wet. In Plaszow, once we were so wet, we needed to sit. That's the first time when we got wet, kind of, because we were just three days there. And then-- oh yeah, yeah. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you finally arrived in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen, we came. We-- yeah-- well, it was the B block. And right beside-- I mean, there was-- ours was the last one, kind of, by the-- there was a piece of field, kind of like a big backyard. And then there was the-- the-- the fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were the trees. So what do you call them, like-- like-- I can't remember names-- like-- like the Christmas trees, you know, the fir? Fir trees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- were there. And planes were going around. Once it was kind of bombing from far away. And the corner of our barrack, kind of like-- just at the corner, kind of like from the tremor-- kind of got collapsed. And everybody said, oh, we wish they would throw a bomb on the barrack and kill us. That what we were wishing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were there. And there was the same-- there we slept on the floor, again, like-- like-- almost like in Munkacs, something like that-- thing that way, and there on the floor. There was nothing-- no climbing, no nothing. Did they have an entrance process, like a shower and the whole thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was water. There was a-- in-- in the thing. But most of the time it was locked. And then we start to get typhoid fever. And-- and there was dysentery, you know, because people were going to that thing. There was outside, too, a thing, but we start to get sick. We were full of lice. We were-- and the things, they had tails. That's how big they were. And-- and what it was there, kinda like the-- the-- that bathroom, like the hallway. You couldn't get into it. It was impossible. And then people start to die, especially the ones that they say-- I was the first one, kind of, from our group who got typhoid fever. I remember once, I-- when I was sick, I-- I dreamt of my-- my parents, that they giving me the cultured milk, you know. And that's kind of sourish. It was so good, you know. Well, anyway, on the zahlappell, it was terrible. It was raining, too. It was in-- in the spring. And-- and I was-- at that time, I was in the middle. And the girls were a thing, because, you know, I-- I couldn't stay on my feet. And-- so they were-- when-- when the woman came, the SS woman, to count-- so one would-- you know, my-- my cousin was standing in the front, and she grabbed me backwards, you know, by my-- what I had on. And-- and the girl-- the-- my niece-- was holding me in the back for that few seconds which she passed by. And then again, I slumped. I-- I was sitting then, because it wasn't wet. And I had the-- a big thing with me, a-- a pot, kind of-- a bowl. I was sitting on that bowl. I shouldn't sit in-- in the thing. So we been sitting there. It's-- it was-- they said that it's coming to an end. You know, there was always rumors. Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had another name there. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was the name there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fortune teller, because in the-- there, in the other end of the barrack, there were the same people, which-- we were together in Guben. There was a mother with a daughter, too-- Hungarian. And-- and-- and they kept on-- are we gonna-- what's gonna happen? And I kept-- I kept on telling lies for them, you know. I said, I heard that the sky is nearing the end. And it's going to be better. And how many times did someone here say-- then it was allowed to walk around between the thing. And they said, [NON-ENGLISH]. That's kind of a fortune woman. Come on, tell us something. Do you heard something-- every-- the thing-- things like that. You know, we tried to cheer ourselves up. And then, I-- when I felt a little bit better from-- from the typhoid fever, I-- while I-- again, start volunteering. Because they said, you know, as people start dying terrible from the typhoid, oh! I remember a thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a very-- she was a very kind of-- with me-- kind as a-- a woman. And as we were sleeping-- oh, we were fighting for sleeping, because we didn't have a place inside to sleep. So in the corridor, we-- we reserved places, you know? Fast-- as soon as we was allowed to get out, we spread that blanket, you know? And we were guarding it all the time. They had it. Now somebody else shouldn't come and take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was always kind of like, clinging to us. And as we were sleeping there-- and she was kind of by the wall. She was laying this way. I-- we were laying this way and she this way. And she was-- we saw that she is dying. She kept on calling my name all night long, all night long. And by-- by the-- the morning, she passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was like, if we wanted to go, they said, there is water in that kind of thing. And so and-- and when it start to get dark, there was a little hole right by that-- where that water latrine-- I mean-- bathroom was. They put the dead people there till morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was other things. There were people who died in-- as they were sleeping in that hall. It's a horrible thing. And if I wanted to go-- I remember when-- I don't know the other girls, when they went. I remember myself. They were naked already, because they were taken off their-- their-- their clothes from them there. I don't know why. I was not at that time in the Totenkommando. And I-- I was still kind with the typhoid. And it's-- you fell in between them, you know. I couldn't get up, because the body kind of is-- it's kind of like a slush. And you step this way. And you step that way. And you kind of-- I-- I can't explain it-- that-- that flesh. And kind of-- so either you got there or you came back. Because it was full. Everybody was laying, laying.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then-- then it accumulated a lot of things, and outside things. So the [NON-ENGLISH] again, said there, who wants to-- to volunteer? The Totenkommando gonna get a bowl of soup more. Totenkommando means death corpse?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the corpses. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: To deliver the corpses.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to deliver there on that big pile. So naturally, we-- the two Romanian girls, and my cousin, Dora, and I, did it. And-- and there was already-- it-- it was just as long as-- as-- as a person, but it has two big wheels in the thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we put the corpse on the top. So that one wheel kind of other balance this way or that way. So you just have to go up, and you step on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by the time before we take them out, you know, and there was a little step. And one got by one foot, the other one by the other foot-- the four of us, you know, by the hand, you know, we were weak. And the neck was kind of banging on the floor and on those steps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were some people which we knew on there, I think. So what do we know? We know that-- I remember from home, if when they were dressing the-- the-- the, you know, somebody died in the village, we were children. And we were sneaking in-- you know, what are they doing? You know, we're curious. And-- or they were telling at home, that what they do. Everybody who ever does there begs [NON-ENGLISH]. Beg forgiveness.

speaker: interviewee
text: Forgiveness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Forgiveness.

speaker: interviewee
text: They ask forgiveness from the person if sometimes I-- I did something, said to you something, you know. So-- and I said, we said-- with-- they were not so religious, the-- the two Romanians. Or maybe they didn't know. I don't know. And I said for her, I think that's what we should do. And we just said, forgive us what we're doing. We can't do it otherwise.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we put them out on the-- on the wagon. And we went to-- stepped on them on the wagon, because we had to pull over. And we had to put on about four of them on that wagon-- at least. So-- and it was downhill a little bit to the-- to go over there where that big, big pile of the dead people were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a house-- just kind of like-- like a pyramid, you know-- not a house, a squarish. But this was kind of, like, a ruin, you know, like a pyramid. And it was so high already that we could not put that kind of like, you know, in the thing. And the SS woman was with us. And we had to, you know, to do like this with a, you know, swing, and then give it a toss. Well, we were not so strong. So sometimes they were just rolling down. Sometimes she just gave us with that stick a little nudge too. And we had to pick it up again, and again till, kind of, it got stuck kind of here. If it rolled down a little bit-- so then we did with the hand, kind of just should got-- get stuck there. So we got a little bit more soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times did you have to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, till it was finished, our barrack. This was just from our barrack we were talking a moment--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just our barrack. And there were other barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, was it-- was it for days, or weeks, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was going on. It was over there. There there's so many people died there. There was a lot of people died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of it was from typhoid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything together-- everything together. There was no crematorium. We went once there, down. I think she was there. I asked her. She said she was in Bergen-Belsen, but she doesn't remember me visiting her with the thing. Because sometimes we was allowed to go. And she was there, too-- way down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heard that there is one of-- from those villages. So we went again-- went to visit her there. That was you know who? I don't know if you know her, Leah-- Moishe [NON-ENGLISH]-- Moishe [NON-ENGLISH]'s mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she went away. She was liberated somewheres else in between. I don't know how that came about. And so we went there. And that was not far away from the end, from the do thing. And there were ruins of bricks and all kinds of material. And they said that that was a crematorium there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- when we were there, there was no crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know how they disposed of the bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they disposed of those things, because the war was ended. And the Germans put them in the-- in the graves-- then, when they came there, you know, to look at them-- what we just saw on-- on the television, you know-- like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your cousin and your nieces, they were with you throughout the entire time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- all the time-- all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how did you know that you had typhoid? Did you have any medical attention? Did someone tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but they were talking, you know. Everybody said there it's, you know. They were educated women, you know, and they said, that's typhoid fever, you know. We were in temperature.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your cousin had it and your two nieces?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- yeah, yeah. But I was the first from my group.

speaker: interviewer
text: No medication?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no water, no nothing-- nothing, nothing, nothing. As a matter of fact, they were chasing-- we couldn't get out of the barrack, you know. It was very slow from that floor in the morning for the zahlappell. She had kind of like a little broom from-- from sticks that she would hit and chase them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to work, even-- or did you have to get up, even when you had a fever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, we had to get up. The zahlappell was all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the fever?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a fever-- there-- there was not such thing-- nothing, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say, 'zahlain', you mean the accounting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zahl, yeah, the-- the counting thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They always accounted every morning--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was always twice-a-day counting. And every--

speaker: interviewer
text: Morning and night?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in every camp. And if-- don't-- in case of something-- somebody was missing from that group, you know, every barrack had their own counting. And if there was not enough, you know, I mean if they made a mistake or something, you could stay there. If they said-- in Plaszow, you're staying till-- till-- I don't know how long-- the middle of the night-- till they found out somebody was-- I don't know what it was. But they said that they-- the counting does not-- kind of-- in Yiddish, they say, [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The typhoid fever-- how long approximately did it last? Was it days, weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks,

speaker: interviewer
text: Weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: A week or so. Well, the first-- the first week was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you feel with the typhoid, besides the fever?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just it hurt, everything. And you are-- and I couldn't get up. I couldn't get up with the headaches.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had to get up anyways.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to get up. I had to get up. There was no way, no way. I had to get up. It didn't work there, nothing-- those things. And we had the-- the-- the diarrhea there took from-- from nothing. It's-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] What-- what kept you going during that period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my two nieces-- I had hopes for the father. At that time, we know already that whoever went to Auschwitz is gone. But their father was, too, in the working camps. And I had two brothers, the one from Prague-- and that I have in the picture-- and Leiser-- [NON-ENGLISH] and Leiser. They went there. And they were, you know, for the Hungarian-- behind the Hungarian front. And they were by the Russian border. And by the Russian border, they sent-- they escaped to Russia. You know, they thought that Russia is our friend, you know. So they escaped to Russia. And there was one-- a guy from another village, which knew them. And he brought us a few words-- not to worry. We are by-- I don't remember who-- what, he didn't said Russia. But so we knew that-- no, yeah. We had-- now I remember. We had, in-- in Munkacs, we knew a guy. And he was a-- a communist. And he went-- you know, some of the communists went to Russia when it just started, kinda-- the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he wrote, he is not where [NON-ENGLISH] is, from Munkacs, you know, from Munkacs. So we knew that he is in Russia. Well, we are in-- we were in seventh heaven that they are in Russia. They are not already under the Germans. So we thought that they gonna come home. But what do the Russians do? They don't believe for nobody. They kept all those-- which, running away. So which they catched from whoever, you know-- thing. They went Siberia-- they put them. So I don't know how long they were there in Siberia, or what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was wanting-- no, wanting that, you know, that there was a Czech legion. When-- when the Russians start going against Hitler, you know, when they were not anymore friends with them, there was a lot of Jewish boys-- even not Jewish-- you know, which formed a Czech legion in Russia. And they went to help for the Russian soldiers. They had their own whatever. You know, I don't know who was in command. The Russians were in command. But there was a lot. And my two brothers were in that legion too. But the one, [NON-ENGLISH] you know, from Prague, he was a very kind of delicate. He was kind of like a city boy, because he went away from home by 18.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to school, to Prague. And he remained there. Oh he took till he was-- I don't know. Was he 30, 35, something, when Hitler-- the things? Well, he was born in '11. And the war was '40, you know. He was already in the years.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not married. And he was the only one not married. And I wasn't married-- with me. And so he was kind of like a little delicate person. And he got tuberculosis. And he didn't-- really, you know. I did found out after the war. Because there was, from our village, was the guy between them, a Jewish guy. As he was-- you know, they were in-- in the basements some years. And he was very sick. And he passed away there in the-- that basements. So it was in Russia, in the middle of the way when they were marching against Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other one was there for-- you saw the soldier? You know, as my husband said, he could have lifted-- a big man, a strong one. He died a awful, horrible death.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was frozen, but he was alive. Pieces were falling off him-- hands, the nose, the ears. But he was walking. He was walking till he-- he could with the Czech legion, And-- [CRYING] --till-- I don't know how long. But as they say, as he said in Yiddish that he was dying alive. Pieces were falling off him.

speaker: interviewer
text: This you found out afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was funny the way I found it-- you know, if-- found that out. When I came to Prague already-- you know, well-- I'm gonna have to go back after the-- to talk back to-- with the-- to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Prague already, after the liberation-- and then everybody is, you know, running around like mad. Do you see this one? I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you see that one? Do you see-- all of a sudden, I hear, there is Moise [NON-ENGLISH] Rosenbaum. And he was a cousin of my sister-in-law. And-- so I found him. And I-- he was in the Czech legion. And he said, yes, I was with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, well, where are they? He said, well, I don't know. I got words from them. Maybe they are somewheres here. I don't know where they are. That's what he told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when two weeks-- till we-- we stopped in Budapest afterwards. And-- and we were going on the train. And I'm sitting on top, going already towards our village-- so in Munkacs-- till Munkacs came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when in Munkacs, we stopped with the train. And I found there a-- a cousin's son, Srule. And-- and I asked him, because-- Srule, do they give you-- you know-- did you see [NON-ENGLISH] or Leiser somewheres?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looks at me, you know. And then he says to me, listen. Sooner or later, you're gonna find out anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how you found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I broke out in--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number six.
